issues.comment: Missing `$` anchor in domain regex — whitelist bypass / blacklist false positives. The regex `@(#{domains})` is not anchored at the end with `$`. When the setting is a pipe-separated domain list like `"example.com|mail.com"`, the regex becomes `/@(example\.com|mail\.com)/i`. If `email_domains_whitelist` is set to `"example.com"`, an attacker can register with `user@example.com.evil.com` — the regex finds `@example.com` as a substring match and passes validation, even though the actual domain is `evil.com`. For blacklists, a legitimate email like `user@spam.com.legitimate.org` would be incorrectly blocked. The fix should anchor the regex with `$`, e.g. `regexp = Regexp.new("@(.+\\.)?(#{domains})$", true)`.

`BlockedEmail.should_block?` has write side-effects during read-only validation. This method is called from within `EmailValidator#validate_each`, meaning it runs during `User#valid?` — a nominally read-only check. Every call to `user.valid?` (or any validation trigger, including failed saves due to other validation errors like username) will increment `match_count` and update `last_match_at` in the database. This corrupts the statistics: the count reflects how many times validation ran, not how many actual blocked registration attempts occurred.
